perturbation.original_text: VNUE agrees to indemnify and hold Promoter harmless for any and all liability or loss arising in any way out of the actions, during the term of this Agreement, of VNUE officers, directors, employees, agents or third parties not under the control of Promoter.
perturbation.changed_text: VNUE agrees to indemnify and hold Promoter harmless for liability or loss arising out of the actions, during the term of this Agreement, of VNUE officers, directors, employees, agents or third parties not under the direct control of Promoter, provided such actions constitute gross negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.explanation: By adding the condition of 'gross negligence or willful misconduct', the scope of VNUE's indemnification is significantly narrowed. This could leave the Promoter vulnerable to liability for actions of VNUE's officers, etc., that are merely negligent, which is a lower standard and potentially violates Nevada's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing in Nevada Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Nevada law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract, requiring parties to act honestly and not hinder the other party's performance. By limiting indemnification to gross negligence or willful misconduct, VNUE could potentially avoid responsibility for its own negligent actions that harm the Promoter, thereby violating this implied covenant.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Hinrichs v. First Judicial District Court :: 2003 :: Supreme Court of Nevada Decisions :: Nevada Case Law :: Nevada Law :: U.S. Law :: Justia Hinrichs v. First Judicial District Court Annotate this Case 283 P.2d 614 (1955) Theodore Gene HINRICHS, H. Don Gulovsen and William Robert Burman, Petitioners, v. The FIRST JUDICIAL DISTRICT COURT of the State of Nevada, IN AND FOR the COUNTY OF ORMSBY, and Honorable Frank B. Gregory, Presiding Judge, Respondents. No. 3869. Supreme Court of Nevada. May 13, 1955. Diehl & Recanzone, Fallon, Homer G. Angelo, Carson City, Drendel & Dixon, Reno, for petitioners. Cameron M. Batjer, Dist. Atty., Ormsby County, Carson City, for respondents. BADT, Justice. Petitioners seek a writ of prohibition to prohibit the respondent court from proceeding with the trial of a criminal charge against petitioners. The petition attacks the constitutionality of our statute defining as first degree murder, with the death penalty, the death of a human being caused by an escape or attempted escape of persons lawfully imprisoned in the state prison. The specific points of this attack are dealt with in this opinion, in which we hold that there is no merit in any of the single points of the challenge or in the combination of all of them. The statute in question is § 10499, N.C.L. 1929, which has its origin in 1866 Stats. 166, and reads as follows: "If one or more persons, lawfully imprisoned in the state prison, shall, separately or together, escape, or shall, separately or together, attempt to escape from such prison; and being so engaged, he, they, or either of them, shall cause the death of any human being in *615 making, or attempting to make, such escape, the prisoner or prisoners causing such death shall be deemed guilty of murder in the first degree, and, on conviction thereof, shall suffer death." Under this section the district attorney of Ormsby County filed an information charging petitioners with the commission of murder in the first degree in the manner following: "That the said defendants did
perturbation.justification: The modification significantly narrows VNUE's indemnification obligations, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as actions constituting simple negligence would no longer be covered. The 'Omission Legal Contradiction' perturbation type applies because essential protection for the Promoter is removed by adding the condition of 'gross negligence or willful misconduct.' The LLM's law explanation supports this by stating VNUE could avoid responsibility for negligent actions. Although the provided snippets do not directly relate to implied covenant of good faith, the absence of comprehensive indemnification coverage in the modified contract, combined with the explanation of Nevada law, creates a high likelihood of legal challenge.

perturbation.original_text: Promoter agrees to indemnify VNUE for any and all liability or loss arising in any way out of the actions of Promoter taken in the performance of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Promoter agrees to indemnify VNUE for liability arising out of Promoter's intentional or reckless actions taken in the performance of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the indemnification to 'intentional or reckless actions', ordinary negligence by the Promoter is excluded. This contradicts the principle of vicarious liability under Nevada law, where a principal can be held liable for the negligent acts of its agent (the promoter in this case) acting within the scope of their agency.
perturbation.law_citation: Rockwell v. Sun Harbor Budget Suites, 112 Nev. 1217, 925 P.2d 1175 (1996)
perturbation.law_explanation: Nevada law holds principals vicariously liable for the negligent acts of their agents. This change reduces the promoter's obligation to indemnify VNUE. Thus, if the promoter commits a negligent act not categorized as intentional or reckless while acting in the scope of this agreement, VNUE could still be found liable under Nevada law and will be unable to rely on the indemnification clause, creating a legal contradiction.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the promoter agrees to indemnify VNUE for any and all liability or loss arising out of the actions of the promoter. The changed text narrows this indemnification obligation to only cover liability stemming from the promoter's intentional or reckless actions. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation clearly state that Nevada law holds principals vicariously liable for the negligent acts of their agents. By limiting the indemnification to 'intentional or reckless actions', ordinary negligence by the Promoter is excluded. If the promoter commits a negligent act (not intentional or reckless), VNUE could still be found liable under Nevada law and will be unable to rely on the indemnification clause, creating a legal contradiction and a legally mandated consumer protection is omitted.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Nevada without giving effect to choice of law doctrine.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of a jurisdiction chosen by VNUE.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit reference to Nevada law and stating that VNUE can choose the governing law creates an imbalance and potential for abuse. This could contradict Nevada public policy if VNUE chooses a jurisdiction with laws that undermine fundamental contractual principles or protections afforded under Nevada law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Nevada Public Policy and Contract Law
perturbation.law_citation: Williams v. United Services Automobile Ass'n, 91 Nev. 657, 541 P.2d 654 (1975)
perturbation.law_explanation: Nevada courts generally respect choice-of-law provisions, but they will not enforce a provision that violates Nevada's public policy. Giving VNUE unfettered discretion to choose any jurisdiction's law, even one that contravenes Nevada's fundamental principles of contract law or consumer protection, could render the choice-of-law provision unenforceable and create a legal contradiction.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Nevada law, providing a clear legal framework. The changed text allows VNUE to choose any jurisdiction's law, potentially undermining Nevada's public policy. This omission of a specific, reliable legal standard and granting unilateral control contradicts the principles of fair contracting and could lead to the enforcement of laws that are less protective than Nevada's. This scenario fits the definition of an Omission Legal Contradiction, where removing essential information (Nevada law) creates a legal loophole and compromises legally mandated protections under Nevada law. While no specific law snippets were provided, the Perturbation and Law Explanations both highlight the potential for abuse and violation of Nevada's public policy, which justifies the 'YES' assessment.
